Title: Engineering DMNT emission in cotton enhances direct and indirect defense against mirid bugs

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively converts the sesquiterpene precursor (E)-nerolidol toward formation of the homoterpene DMNT
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qPCR; volatile metabolite profiling by GC-MS; Sanger sequencing for edited alleles; no public omics accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CRISPR/Cas9 construct using 35S-driven Cas9 in knockout generation; overexpression promoter not clearly stated in the text (NA)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: herbivore-infested aerial tissues, especially leaves/plant volatile emission
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRISPR knockout of GhCYP82L1 reduced herbivore-induced DMNT emission relative to controls. 2. Co-knockout with GhCYP82L2 abolished detectable DMNT emission. 3. Overexpression of GhCYP82L1 increased DMNT emission, and co-overexpression with GhTPS14 caused a much larger increase. 4. Elevated DMNT emission was associated with stronger attraction of mirid bugs and female parasitoids, while high DMNT levels were toxic to bugs.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Volatile defense compound involved in direct toxicity to mirid bugs and indirect defense by attracting parasitoid wasps
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Apolygus lucorum infestation; methyl jasmonate induction; methyl salicylate tested but ineffective for DMNT induction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In overexpression lines, transcript abundance was highest at flowering stage after bug infestation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Homoterpene branch derived from sesquiterpene precursor oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher DMNT emission increased attraction of A. lucorum, potentially creating attraction before toxic effects; no obvious adverse growth effects reported for edited plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 knockout; overexpression; qPCR; GC-MS volatile profiling; insect behavioral assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after A. lucorum infestation; highest transcript level at flowering stage in overexpression lines
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported genetically but formal rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for engineering inducible high-DMNT cotton for pest management, especially when stacked with GhTPS14
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single knockout reduced but did not eliminate DMNT, indicating functional redundancy with GhCYP82L2

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidatively converts the sesquiterpene precursor (E)-nerolidol toward formation of the homoterpene DMNT
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qPCR; volatile metabolite profiling by GC-MS; Sanger sequencing for edited alleles; no public omics accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: CRISPR/Cas9 construct using 35S-driven Cas9 in knockout generation; overexpression promoter not clearly stated in the text (NA)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: herbivore-infested aerial tissues, especially leaves/plant volatile emission
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRISPR knockout of GhCYP82L2 reduced herbivore-induced DMNT emission relative to controls. 2. Co-knockout with GhCYP82L1 abolished detectable DMNT emission. 3. Overexpression of GhCYP82L2 increased DMNT emission, and co-overexpression with GhTPS14 further enhanced emission. 4. Functional overlap with GhCYP82L1 supports redundant contribution to DMNT biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Volatile defense compound involved in direct toxicity to mirid bugs and indirect defense by attracting parasitoid wasps
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Apolygus lucorum infestation; methyl jasmonate induction; methyl salicylate tested but ineffective for DMNT induction
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: In overexpression lines, transcript abundance was highest at flowering stage after bug infestation
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Homoterpene branch derived from sesquiterpene precursor oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Higher DMNT emission may increase attraction of A. lucorum while high dose can be toxic; no obvious adverse growth effects reported for edited plants
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: CRISPR/Cas9 knockout; overexpression; qPCR; GC-MS volatile profiling; insect behavioral assays
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after A. lucorum infestation; highest transcript level at flowering stage in overexpression lines
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: key control step supported genetically but formal rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful target for engineering high-DMNT cotton, particularly in stacked constructs with GhTPS14
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Single knockout reduced but did not eliminate DMNT, indicating functional redundancy with GhCYP82L1

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Produces the sesquiterpene precursor (E)-nerolidol used for downstream DMNT formation
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: qPCR; volatile metabolite profiling by GC-MS; no public omics accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Overexpression promoter not clearly stated in the text (NA)
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: herbivore-infested aerial tissues, especially leaves/plant volatile emission
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Overexpression of GhTPS14 alone significantly increased DMNT emission. 2. Co-overexpression of GhTPS14 with GhCYP82L1 gave the highest DMNT emission, reaching about 10–15 fold above wild type at flowering. 3. GhTPS14 transcript level was significantly induced by MeJA, linking jasmonate signaling to enhanced DMNT production. 4. Highest expression occurred at flowering stage after infestation.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Volatile defense compound involved in direct toxicity to mirid bugs and indirect defense by attracting parasitoid wasps
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Apolygus lucorum infestation; methyl jasmonate induction; methyl salicylate ineffective
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in leaves after infestation, with highest level at flowering stage; induced by MeJA
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Sesquiterpene precursor supply branch for DMNT homoterpene formation
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated DMNT can attract A. lucorum despite later toxic effects
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Overexpression; qPCR; GC-MS volatile profiling; hormone induction assay; insect behavioral assays via co-overexpression lines
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies precursor and increases DMNT emission in cotton
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaves after A. lucorum infestation; highest at flowering stage; upregulated by MeJA
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: important upstream control point but formal rate-limiting status not established
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong engineering target for boosting DMNT, especially stacked with GhCYP82L1 or GhCYP82L2
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper cites previous work identifying GhTPS14 as a de novo DMNT biosynthetic enzyme and experimentally tests its overexpression here
